states.question: What does the Cook-Levin Theorem prove?
choices: B: That Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) is NP-complete.
C: That Dijkstra's algorithm is optimal.
states.explanation: Cook and Levin proved that SAT is NP-complete, showing that any NP problem can be translated to a SAT formula in polynomial time.

states.analogy_description: You have a board of light bulbs and switches connected by logical gates. A formula specifies conditions. SAT is the problem of finding a configuration of ON/OFF switches to turn all lights ON.

whiteboard_data.title: Clause Evaluation Node
whiteboard_data.description: Checking truth value of OR-ed boolean literals.

steps.details: Evaluating clause with assignments x1=False, x2=False
not x2 is True. Clause is satisfied.

states.exercise_description: A clause is a list of integers where positive index means literal, and negative index means negated literal. Write `evaluate_clause(assignment, clause)` checking if clause evaluates to `True`.
Write a simple backtracking SAT solver `solve_sat_backtracking(clauses, variables)` that returns a dictionary assignment if satisfiable, or `None` if not.

states.prompt: Explain Satisfiability in your own words to a peer. Use an analogy from daily life.

flashcards.back: Conjunctive Normal Form (AND of OR clauses).
A version of SAT where every clause contains exactly 3 literals. It is also NP-complete.
